2604.09364.title: Arbitration Failure, Not Perceptual Blindness: How Vision-Language Models Resolve Visual-Linguistic Conflicts
2604.09364.paper_summary: When a Vision-Language Model (VLM) sees a blue banana and answers "yellow", is the problem of perception or arbitration? We explore the question in ten VLMs with various sizes and reveal an Encoding--Grounding Dissociation: models that fail to report what they see (and thus provide a wrong answer) still encode the visual evidence as strongly as models that provide the correct answer. Using Multimodal Arbitration Crossover (MAC) analysis with layer-by-layer Logit Lens probing, we track the competition between visual and prior signals across every layer of each model. We show that visual attributes can be linearly decodable from early layers (AUC > 0.86). The accuracy remains nearly identical for both successful and failed samples. However, the gap in the final-layer logit -- not the strength of encoding -- better predicts grounding outcomes with a correlation of . After having studied when VLMs base their answers on image clues rather than prior knowledge, we want to understand the causal relationships. We establish causality through full-sequence activation patching. The standard last-token interventions in LLM interpretability do not affect VLMs. In contrast, replacing the full token sequence at layers identified by MAC alters 60 to 84% of outputs. Partial-token decomposition shows that image tokens carry almost all of the causal impact, while text tokens have none. Scaling addresses the remaining architectural differences to achieve perfect retention. Moving from diagnosis to intervention, we show that training-free activation steering -- both linear and sparse autoencoder-guided -- in early layers can improve visual grounding by up to +3.8% with degrading performance in some setups. Overall, these findings lead to a clear conclusion: VLMs already see well, but the challenge is acting on what they see. Targeted interventions can help to bridge this gap.
2604.09364.paper_summary_zh: 當一個視覺-語言模型（VLM）看到一根藍色的香蕉並回答「黃色」，這是感知還是仲裁的問題？我們在十個不同大小的VLM中探討這個問題，並揭示了一個編碼-基礎分離現象：那些未能報告所見（因此提供錯誤答案）的模型，仍然像提供正確答案的模型一樣強烈地編碼視覺證據。通過使用多模態仲裁交叉（MAC）分析，並進行逐層的Logit Lens探測，我們追蹤每個模型每一層中視覺信號與先前信號之間的競爭。我們顯示視覺屬性可以從早期層線性解碼（AUC > 0.86）。成功樣本和失敗樣本的準確性幾乎相同。然而，最終層logit的差距——而不是編碼的強度——更好地預測了基礎結果，相關性為。經過研究VLM何時基於圖像線索而非先前知識來給出答案後，我們希望理解因果關係。我們通過全序列激活修補建立因果關係。LLM可解釋性中的標準最後令牌干預不會影響VLM。相反，在MAC識別的層中替換整個令牌序列會改變60%到84%的輸出。部分令牌分解顯示圖像令牌幾乎承載了所有的因果影響，而文本令牌則沒有。縮放解決了剩餘的架構差異，以實現完美保留。從診斷轉向干預，我們顯示無需訓練的激活引導——包括線性和稀疏自編碼器引導——在早期層中可以使視覺基礎提高多達+3.8%，但在某些設置中性能會下降。總體而言，這些發現得出了一個明確的結論：VLM已經能夠很好地看見，但挑戰在於如何根據所見行動。針對性的干預可以幫助彌補這一差距。
2604.09364.author: Farhad Nooralahzadeh et.al.
2604.09364.authors: Farhad Nooralahzadeh, Omid Rohanian, Yi Zhang, Jonathan Fürst, Kurt Stockinger
